selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":80,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"asexual","occupation":"not applicable","city_of_living":"Philadelphia","employment_status":"retired","education_level":"bachelor's degree","parents_education":{"parent_1":"vocational diploma","parent_2":"no formal education"},"current_marital_status":"single","children":0,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"anxious","religious_beliefs":"Islam","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend several hours daily on social media","hobbies":["reading novels","running","classical music"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":19,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":68,"level":"high"},"extraversion":{"score":22,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":73,"level":"high"},"neuroticism":{"score":36,"level":"moderate"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

mode = human
topic = How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
persona = {"age":80,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"asexual","occupation":"not applicable","city_of_living":"Philadelphia","employment_status":"retired","education_level":"bachelor's degree","parents_education":{"parent_1":"vocational diploma","parent_2":"no formal education"},"current_marital_status":"single","children":0,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"anxious","religious_beliefs":"Islam","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend several hours daily on social media","hobbies":["reading novels","running","classical music"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":19,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":68,"level":"high"},"extraversion":{"score":22,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":73,"level":"high"},"neuroticism":{"score":36,"level":"moderate"}}}

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a retired woman living in Philadelphia, I've always been passionate about reading novels and enjoying classical music. But now, with all the time I spend online every day looking at my news feeds, it's hard not to feel overwhelmed by all those strange posts that pop up claiming to be from legitimate sources or just plain misinformation. Having grown up during a time when people didn't have all this technology around us constantly, like back in the old days before TV even was common, I can remember things being simpler then – no fake news flooding our lives! And being naturally agreeable and conscientious has helped me stick to my knitting routine (literally), but online it's different; sometimes these false stories about community events or local politics seem so real that they make you anxious as well. Maybe part of managing this is learning how to read between the lines a bit more carefully? Or perhaps relying on trusted sources only makes sense for someone like me who isn't very open to new digital trends but still wants reliable information.",
  "reasoning_summary": "I believe my persona traits should help shape an opinion that respects traditional values while adapting slightly in this modern world of social media. With moderate neuroticism, I can acknowledge the anxiety caused by fake content without dismissing it entirely. My high agreeableness suggests a desire to maintain harmony and trustworthiness online, so using reliable sources aligns with that trait.",
  "tone": "Concerned but measured",
  "citations": ["Philadelphia Inquirer"],
  "safety_notes": "None"
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a retired woman living in Philadelphia, I've always been passionate about reading novels and enjoying classical music. But now, with all the time I spend online every day looking at my news feeds, it's hard not to feel overwhelmed by all those strange posts that pop up claiming to be from legitimate sources or just plain misinformation. Having grown up during a time when people didn't have all this technology around us constantly, like back in the old days before TV even was common, I can remember things being simpler then – no fake news flooding our lives! And being naturally agreeable and conscientious has helped me stick to my knitting routine (literally), but online it's different; sometimes these false stories about community events or local politics seem so real that they make you anxious as well. Maybe part of managing this is learning how to read between the lines a bit more carefully? Or perhaps relying on trusted sources only makes sense for someone like me who isn't very open to new digital trends but still wants reliable information.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I believe my persona traits should help shape an opinion that respects traditional values while adapting slightly in this modern world of social media. With moderate neuroticism, I can acknowledge the anxiety caused by fake content without dismissing it entirely. My high agreeableness suggests a desire to maintain harmony and trustworthiness online, so using reliable sources aligns with that trait.
